sector: Nuclear fuel cycle / uranium enrichment (HALEU) — scale-out Basket E (POWER-GEN), the fuel-supply node of the AI-power build-out
bull_thesis: Centrus is the only U.S.-owned, U.S.-technology producer of HALEU (high-assay low-enriched uranium, the fuel that advanced reactors require), and it holds the Department of Energy's HALEU Operation Contract at its Piketon, Ohio facility, having already 'contractually produced over 1.6 metric tons of HALEU UF6 for the government' as of the first quarter of 2026. HALEU is the single hardest physical bottleneck for the advanced-reactor and small modular reactor layer of the AI-power build-out: every next-generation SMR (small modular reactor) needs domestic HALEU, and Centrus is effectively the sole Western supply chain — Russia's TENEX is the only other source, and it is now sanctioned and banned from U.S. commerce. The franchise is being de-risked on several fronts at once. First, a $900 million DOE task order (with potential to exceed $1 billion) funds commercial-scale capacity on a milestone basis, providing another pool of low-cost capital. Second, a $560 million Oak Ridge centrifuge-manufacturing build-out re-industrializes the supply chain and represents, in management's words, a once-in-a-generation opportunity to reclaim American leadership. Third, the company carries a $3.9 billion backlog through 2040, including $2.4 billion in uranium enrichment under definitive agreements. Fourth, a letter of intent signed on June 18, 2026 with Oklo covers HALEU supply for up to five Aurora powerhouses on a 1.2-gigawatt Ohio campus, with potential Oklo prepayments to fund the build. The balance sheet is genuinely strong: roughly $1.9 billion in unrestricted cash, approximately $660 million in net cash (cash minus debt), financed with 0% and 2.25% convertible notes, and management did not draw on its equity offering program despite having one available — so the scale-up is funded without forced share issuance. A Palantir partnership has flagged roughly $300 million of potential cost savings. The thesis holds if HALEU commercial demand materializes on schedule (2029 and beyond), the $900 million DOE order converts to a funded definitive agreement at acceptable margin, and the build stays largely non-dilutive. If those conditions hold, Centrus owns a regulatory-licensed Western monopoly on the fuel that gates the entire SMR and advanced-nuclear power tier feeding AI data centers.
bear_thesis: The stock is pricing a 2029 and beyond opportunity as near-certain today. Current revenue is flat to declining — full-year 2025 at $448.7 million, up only 1.5%; trailing twelve months at $452.3 million, down 4.1%. Operating margin is thin (roughly 6.7% on a trailing twelve-month basis). First-quarter 2026 net income fell 63% year over year ($10.0 million versus $27.2 million) on contract timing. Most importantly, free cash flow has turned negative (negative $61.4 million trailing twelve months versus positive $31.3 million in full-year 2025) as the build-out begins — this is no longer a self-funding compounder; it is a cash-consuming construction project. Yet the stock trades at roughly 67 times trailing and 73 times forward earnings on a $3.77 billion market capitalization built on that flat revenue base. The two load-bearing catalysts are both non-binding: the $900 million DOE order 'still needs to be finalized through negotiations,' and the Oklo deal is a letter of intent with deliveries not starting until 2029 and only potential prepayments 'to be further negotiated in a future definitive agreement.' The risky window is the 2026 to 2028 period: flat revenue, negative free cash flow, and capital requirements arrive before any commercial HALEU cash. Management itself says further build-out is 'tied to securing additional firm customer orders and capital resources' — a signal that larger, potentially dilutive raises may come. The stock is already a momentum casualty, down roughly 59% from its $464 fifty-two-week high to roughly $191, so the AI-power scarcity premium has already been priced and partly unwound. An investor today is not buying cheap; they are buying a richly valued, long-dated, single-factor bet — AI capital expenditure durability plus nuclear policy continuity — shared by the entire power-generation basket. An AI-spending pause or an SMR timeline slip impairs the whole thesis at once.
sop_bull: Sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) bull case: roughly $215 to $245 per share. The core uranium enrichment and technical-services operating business, running at roughly $450 to $500 million in annual revenue, is valued at a premium strategic multiple given its monopoly scarcity — approximately $60 to $75 million in normalized owner earnings multiplied by 22 to 26 times gives roughly $1.5 to $1.7 billion, or about $76 to $86 per share. The HALEU commercial franchise is treated as a real option on the $900 million DOE order, the 12 metric-ton production capacity, the $2.4 billion in definitive uranium enrichment agreements, and the Oklo and SMR pipeline; a risk-adjusted net present value (the present value of future cash flows discounted for risk) of roughly $2.0 to $2.6 billion in the bull case (approximately $100 to $130 per share), crediting Western-monopoly pricing power and DOE and Oklo prepayment de-risking. Net cash of roughly $660 million adds approximately $33 per share. The three pieces sum to roughly $4.2 to $4.8 billion, or approximately $213 to $244 per share across roughly 19.7 million shares.
sop_bear: Sum-of-the-parts bear case: roughly $95 to $120 per share. The core business is valued at a market-to-modest-premium multiple on a flat, lumpy, free-cash-flow-negative base — approximately $55 million in normalized earnings multiplied by 16 to 18 times gives roughly $0.9 to $1.0 billion, or about $46 to $51 per share. The HALEU franchise is heavily discounted for non-binding awards, 2029 and beyond timing, capital intensity, and execution risk, yielding only roughly $0.6 to $1.0 billion of risk-adjusted value (approximately $30 to $50 per share), treating the $900 million DOE order and the Oklo letter of intent as unsigned. Net cash of roughly $660 million, partly consumed by the build (net usable roughly $400 to $500 million), adds about $20 to $25 per share. The three pieces sum to roughly $1.9 to $2.4 billion, or approximately $95 to $120 per share — meaning the stock at roughly $191 already embeds most of the bull-case option value.
sop_breakdown: Base-case reconciliation at roughly $3.0 billion enterprise value across roughly 19.7 million shares, implying approximately $150 per share. The core uranium enrichment and technical-services business contributes roughly $1.2 to $1.4 billion (approximately $65 per share), using a premium-to-market multiple on the roughly $450 to $500 million, monopoly-scarce but flat and free-cash-flow-negative current franchise. The HALEU commercial option, probability-weighted between the bull net present value and the bear haircut, contributes roughly $1.0 to $1.4 billion (approximately $55 per share), crediting the DOE incumbency and Oklo demand while discounting for non-binding awards and 2029 and beyond timing. Net cash of roughly $660 million, net of build-out drawdown (roughly $500 million usable), contributes approximately $28 per share. The three pieces sum to roughly $150 per share. At roughly $191, the market is paying roughly 27% above this base case — that is, the market is underwriting closer to the bull HALEU net present value with limited margin of safety.
risk_reward: The risk-reward balance is unfavorable to balanced at today's price. The path to the base-case fair value runs downward: $191 versus roughly $150 is negative 21%. The bull sum-of-the-parts at roughly $215 to $245 represents only 13% to 28% upside. The bear sum-of-the-parts at roughly $95 to $120 represents 37% to 50% downside. On a probability-weighted basis the asymmetry is negative — roughly one unit of risk-adjusted upside (only in the bull leg) against roughly two units of downside if the HALEU option is discounted or AI capital expenditure wobbles. A materially lower entry price, toward the $120 to $150 range, is where the option is closer to being priced in for free.
catalyst_status: SOFT_EXTENDED. The two load-bearing catalysts are real but non-binding as of 2026-06-22: (1) the $900M DOE HALEU task order (announced Q1-26) 'still needs to be finalized through negotiations' — a milestone-based award, not yet a funded definitive agreement; (2) the Oklo HALEU supply agreement is a LETTER OF INTENT signed 06-18-2026 (8-K filed), supplying up to five Aurora powerhouses with deliveries 'scheduled to begin in 2029' and only POTENTIAL prepayments 'to be further negotiated in a future definitive agreement.' The Oak Ridge $560M build and the $108.2M funded DOE option year are FIRED/hard, but the commercial-scale value driver (definitive $900M+ contract + signed Oklo offtake) is non-binding and long-dated. Net status = SOFT_EXTENDED: genuine, dated demand signals, but the hard conversion is unsigned and the cash flows are 2029+.
peer_comps_note:
True peers are the nuclear fuel-cycle and SMR-fuel layer of the power-generation basket (live multiples verified June 18 to 21, 2026). Cameco (CCJ) at $105.67 trades at roughly 104 times earnings — uranium mining and conversion, the scaled incumbent, profitable, with a broad fuel-cycle business. Oklo (OKLO) at $60.88 trades at a negative price-to-earnings ratio, as it is a pre-revenue SMR developer and is actually Centrus's customer, not a fuel peer. NuScale Power (SMR) at $11.67 is a loss-making SMR-licensing developer. Lightbridge (LTBR) at roughly $9.61 is a pre-revenue advanced-fuel developer with no earnings. LEU at roughly $191 trades at roughly 67 times trailing and 73 times forward earnings.

Where LEU ranks among peers: on valuation it is rich in absolute terms (roughly 70 times) but is the only profitable, cash-generating name in the SMR-fuel cohort aside from Cameco, which is larger and more diversified at roughly 100 times. On growth, current growth is flat (down 4% trailing twelve months), inferior to the pre-revenue ramp narratives of the SMR developers, but Centrus has real revenue today unlike Oklo, NuScale, and Lightbridge. On margins, Centrus has positive operating and net margins, uniquely so versus the loss-making developers, though margins are thin (operating margin roughly 6.7%). On balance-sheet quality, real net income plus a net-cash balance sheet puts Centrus far above the cash-burning developers. On exposure to the AI capital-expenditure theme, Centrus has among the purest and most defensible positions in the whole basket: HALEU is the physical chokepoint and Centrus is the sole U.S.-technology supplier, a Western near-monopoly that is structurally stronger than any single SMR developer. The overall read: LEU is the highest-quality, most monopoly-protected name in the SMR-fuel cohort, but it is not cheap. Its multiple already capitalizes the HALEU option, and the cohort as a whole (Cameco at roughly 100 times, Oklo and NuScale loss-making) shows the entire layer is priced on 2029 and beyond expectations — a shared re-rating risk.
role_in_scaleout: Centrus is the fuel-supply chokepoint for the advanced-reactor and small modular reactor sub-tier of the power-generation basket. As the sole U.S.-owned, U.S.-technology HALEU producer and the DOE incumbent, it is the single Western source of the enriched fuel that every next-generation SMR — including the Oklo Aurora — physically requires to power AI data centers. That demand is highly durable if SMRs deploy at scale beginning in 2029 and beyond, but the timing is the entire risk: today's revenue is flat and the commercial HALEU ramp is years away.
consensus_delta: Where we disagree with the market: the street is not obviously looking the wrong way here — if anything it is looking too far forward. Consensus already credits Centrus as the U.S. HALEU monopoly and capitalizes that into roughly a 70 times forward multiple and a $3.77 billion market capitalization on roughly $450 million of flat revenue. The bullish thesis is consensus, not contrarian. The genuine disagreement with the market is on the downside: the market is pricing the $900 million DOE order and the Oklo letter of intent as near-signed, when both are explicitly non-binding ('still needs to be finalized'; an letter of intent with 2029 deliveries and only 'potential' prepayments). The market is also under-weighting that free cash flow has turned negative (negative $61 million trailing twelve months) — Centrus is now a cash-consuming construction project, not a compounder, and management's 'tied to capital resources' language signals possible future share issuance. The correct contrarian read is not 'this is cheap and misunderstood' but rather 'a real Western-monopoly franchise is being priced at a euphoric multiple on long-dated, unsigned demand, with the easy money already made (stock down 59% from its $464 high yet still roughly 70 times earnings).' Where the street is wrong: assuming the 2029 and beyond HALEU ramp is de-risked enough to justify roughly 70 times today.
valley_of_death: The risky window runs from 2026 through 2028. Concretely: revenue stays flat to lumpy (roughly $450 to $500 million guided for 2026) with no commercial HALEU revenue until approximately 2029 — a multi-year gap between the narrative and the cash. Free cash flow is already negative (negative $61 million trailing twelve months) and will deepen as the $560 million Oak Ridge build and 12 metric-ton capacity spend ramps — capital expenditure was $23 million in the first quarter of 2026 alone and is rising. The $1.9 billion cash hoard funds the build for now, but management's own 'tied to securing additional capital resources' language flags the risk of a larger raise (equity or additional convertible notes) before commercial HALEU cash arrives, which would pressure the roughly 70 times multiple. At the macro level this name's terminal value is a single shared factor with the rest of the power-generation basket — AI capital expenditure durability plus nuclear and SMR policy continuity. An AI-spending guide-down or an SMR timeline slip would compress multiples across the whole cohort at once, and a richly priced, free-cash-flow-negative, long-dated name compresses hardest. There is no debt-maturity wall (convertible notes due 2030 and 2032, net cash position) — the risk is multiple compression and capital calls, not insolvency.
positioning_washout: Forced-selling pressure has partly already played out — LEU is roughly 59% off its $464 fifty-two-week high, so momentum and retail euphoria have already been culled and the stock has round-tripped a large move. There is no dividend or yield base to force-liquidate (no payout). The remaining risk is a thematic washout: the name trades as a high-beta AI-power and nuclear-theme vehicle, heavily owned by thematic exchange-traded funds (nuclear and uranium funds), momentum funds, and retail investors. An AI-spending scare or a single SMR disappointment can trigger correlated, mechanical position reductions across the whole nuclear-theme cohort (Cameco at roughly 100 times earnings, Oklo and NuScale loss-making all ran together on the way up). That is the flush worth waiting for — a basket-wide nuclear and AI-power re-rating that takes LEU back toward the $120 to $150 range, where the HALEU option is closer to being priced in for free.
forcing_function: The catalyst that forces the re-rating, in either direction: first, conversion of the $900 million DOE task order into a signed, funded definitive agreement (management says it 'still needs to be finalized') — a positive re-rating trigger if signed at good margin, a negative one if it slips or prices poorly. Second, a signed Oklo definitive HALEU offtake agreement (the June 18, 2026 item is only a letter of intent), including confirmed prepayments, which would hard-de-risk demand. Third, upcoming earnings prints showing whether free cash flow burn is contained and whether a capital raise is announced (the 'tied to capital resources' signal). Fourth, any AI capital expenditure or hyperscaler spending guide-down — the dominant shared macro factor — which would force a basket-wide re-rating. The asymmetry today is that the positive triggers (signed contracts) are already largely reflected in the price, while the negative ones (capital raise, AI-spending pause, contract slip) are not.
moderator_conclusion: Centrus is a genuinely high-quality, strategically irreplaceable franchise — the sole U.S.-technology HALEU producer, DOE incumbent, net-cash balance sheet, real backlog, and the clearest physical-bottleneck exposure in the entire power-generation basket. The analyst's forensic read is right: the trajectory is strengthening, the moat is narrow but widening, and there is no secular threat (energy transition is a tailwind here, not a headwind). But the expectations-arbitrage analysis and the macro regime lens force a pass at this price. The current regime document explicitly names AI capital expenditure as the dominant shared macro factor (roughly 40% of S&P 500 earnings-per-share growth in 2026) and requires that the drivers of a thesis be independent across holdings at the book level. LEU's entire thesis hinges on the same single shared factor as the rest of the power-generation basket, so it is penalized for correlation rather than rewarded for quality. The load-bearing catalysts are dated but soft with no contractual payout yet — the $900 million DOE order is unsigned; the Oklo deal is a letter of intent with 2029 deliveries. Most of this optionality has already been priced and partly unwound: the stock is down 59% from its $464 high yet still trades at roughly 70 times forward earnings. The base-case sum-of-the-parts fair value is roughly $150 versus a roughly $191 market price — the investor is paying roughly 27% above intrinsic value for a 2029 and beyond option, with downside to roughly $95 to $120 (bear case) far exceeding upside to roughly $215 to $245 (bull case, and only if all catalysts convert). Free cash flow has turned negative and a capital raise is a live risk. The verdict is pass for now at conviction 3 — a real franchise, not a value trap, so not a 1 or 2 — but saturated, richly priced, and correlated to a shared macro factor. The right action is to add to a watchlist and wait for a capitulation toward $120 to $150, where the HALEU option is closer to being priced in for free. On pure valuation and forensic quality alone, stripping away the catalyst and AI overlay, the name is a flat-revenue, free-cash-flow-negative business trading above its sum-of-the-parts fair value: the quality is real, but the investor is over-paying for it. This name belongs in the energy-transition winner category; no secular decline theme applies.
interrogator_dossier:
Centrus Energy Corp. (NYSE: LEU) — Forensic Read as of June 22, 2026

Latest reporting: first quarter 2026 (reported May 5, 2026; earnings call May 7, 2026). Current price roughly $191 (June 18 close), market capitalization roughly $3.77 billion, roughly 19.7 million shares outstanding. Primary source: first-quarter 2026 transcript; multi-quarter arc reconstructed from that call plus reported financials plus dated press materials.

Narrative arc

Centrus has executed a clean pivot from a legacy uranium enrichment unit (SWU, or separative work unit, the measure of effort needed to enrich uranium) trader and contract-services shop into the only U.S.-owned, U.S.-technology HALEU producer, positioned as the domestic fuel chokepoint for the advanced-reactor and small modular reactor build-out. The first-quarter 2026 call is now a story of how fast and how much capital to deploy to scale the centrifuge cascade: the DOE incumbency at Piketon ('contractually produced over 1.6 metric tons of HALEU UF6 for the government'); the $900 million DOE task order ('potential to exceed $1 billion; still needs to be finalized'); the $560 million Oak Ridge centrifuge build ('once-in-a-generation opportunity to reclaim American leadership'); $3.9 billion backlog through 2040 ($2.4 billion in definitive uranium enrichment agreements); and the June 18, 2026 Oklo letter of intent for up to five Aurora powerhouses. The story is expanding, not contracting.

Claims versus financials

Revenue for full-year 2025 was $448.7 million, up 1.5%; trailing twelve months $452.3 million, down 4.1% — flat overall, with the demand story sitting in the forward backlog rather than the current run-rate. The $3.9 billion backlog is credible but long-dated. First-quarter 2026 net income of $10.0 million fell 63% year over year from $27.2 million; operating income was only $0.8 million; trailing twelve-month operating margin is roughly 6.7%, while the 13.4% net margin is flattered by below-the-line items. Balance sheet claims check out: $1.9 billion in cash, roughly $660 million in net cash, management did not draw on the equity offering program, funded by 0% and 2.25% convertible notes. Free cash flow on a trailing twelve-month basis is negative $61.4 million (full-year 2025 was positive $31.3 million) — it has turned negative as the build begins, which refutes the characterization of Centrus as a self-funding compounder.

Management tone and confidence

Rising. Management used offensive language throughout, raised the 2026 revenue guidance to $450 to $500 million from $425 to $475 million, raised Piketon hiring targets to more than 100 from more than 50, and cited the Palantir partnership for roughly $300 million of potential cost savings. The only honest hedge: the $900 million order 'still needs to be finalized,' and further build is 'tied to securing additional firm customer orders and capital resources.'

Guidance credibility

Medium-high, roughly 4 of 5. Management raised guidance within the period; net income and cash are real; the DOE incumbency and 1.6 metric tons already delivered are hard anchors. Reported earnings are lumpy under generally accepted accounting principles — first-quarter net income fell 63% on higher revenue, reflecting contract-timing rather than demand erosion.

Analyst pressure points

When does the $900 million order convert to a funded agreement and at what margin? What total capital does scaling to 12 or more metric tons require, and will it stay non-dilutive? When does HALEU demand actually arrive (Oklo deliveries begin in 2029)? Management was vague on the dollars-and-dates of conversion.

Key signals — positive and negative

Positive: sole U.S.-technology HALEU producer with DOE incumbency (regulatory-license moat); net cash of $660 million funds the build without dilution; $3.9 billion backlog plus the Oklo letter of intent; DOE de-risks the capital stack. Negative: free cash flow is negative (negative $61 million trailing twelve months); roughly 67 times trailing and 73 times forward earnings on flat revenue; the commercial HALEU ramp is 2029 and beyond; the load-bearing items are non-binding; the stock is down roughly 59% from its $464 high.

Hidden signals

The Palantir and '$300 million savings' framing is deliberate repositioning of a centrifuge story in AI-infrastructure language. The capital-discipline language ('progressive, tied to firm orders') caps blue-sky upside but protects the downside.

Capital-allocation verdict

Strengthening trajectory. The most important observation: this is a 2029 and beyond option priced at roughly 70 times forward earnings on flat revenue and negative free cash flow. The thesis is HALEU demand timing plus conversion of non-binding awards, not the run-rate. The moat is built on regulatory licensing, scale cost, and intangible assets (security-cleared U.S.-origin centrifuge technology, sole domestic supplier) — narrow today (thin margins, negative free cash flow, no high stable return on invested capital) but widening (DOE incumbency, only-U.S.-technology designation, and a growing demand pipeline all strengthening). The secular force runs in favor — energy transition and AI-power build-out are tailwinds, not threats. The danger is over-payment for a real but distant option, not terminal decline.

Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory strengthening; moat narrow but real; moat trend widening; secular threat none.
skeptic_kill_fact: There is no clean kill: every load-bearing bear fact verifies against primary sources, so the bearish pass thesis survives. The would-be kill — a hard, signed contract that de-risks the option — does not exist. The $900 million DOE task order 'remains subject to negotiation of a definitive agreement,' and the Oklo deal is an explicit letter of intent with 2029 first deliveries and only 'potential' prepayments 'to be further negotiated in a future definitive agreement.' Both load-bearing catalysts are confirmed non-binding. Free cash flow is confirmed negative (roughly negative $58 million for the quarter: operating cash flow negative $35.1 million on inventory and working-capital builds, capital expenditure $23.2 million). The stock is confirmed roughly 59% off its $464.25 fifty-two-week high yet still at roughly 70 times earnings. The 2029 and beyond option is richly priced and unsigned, exactly as the bear case claims.
skeptic_corrections: One stale data point in the dossier and the bear case: first-quarter 2026 revenue was $76.7 million, up roughly 5% year over year — not flat to declining. The 'flat revenue' framing is a trailing twelve-month artifact; the most recent quarter actually grew. Net income of $10.0 million, down roughly 63% year over year from $27.2 million, is confirmed, but the cause is contract timing and below-the-line items, not demand erosion. The 2026 guidance was raised to $450 to $500 million from $425 to $475 million. The base-case sum-of-the-parts fair value of $150 is built on the correct, current first-quarter 2026 financials and the correct non-binding catalyst status — it is not stale. Net cash of roughly $660 million, $1.9 billion in total cash, $3.9 billion backlog, and the fact that management did not draw on the equity offering program all verify. The net effect of this correction is marginally less bearish on the run-rate (the recent quarter grew), but it does not change the verdict: the investor is still paying roughly 27% above base-case sum-of-the-parts fair value for unsigned 2029 and beyond optionality.
skeptic_evidence: SEC 8-K (Centrus, filed 2026-05-05), Q1-2026 results: revenue $76.7M (+5% YoY), net income $10.0M, FY26 guidance raised to $450-500M; $900.0M DOE task order 'remains subject to negotiation of a definitive agreement' + funded $108.2M option year — https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/0001065059/000162828026030674/ex991-10q2026_05x05.htm

SEC 8-K / PR (Oklo-Centrus, 2026-06-18): Letter of Intent to supply HALEU for up to five Aurora powerhouses (1.2 GW S. Ohio campus), deliveries 'scheduled to begin in 2029,' only 'potential' prepayments 'to be further negotiated in a future definitive agreement' — https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/oklo-centrus-sign-letter-of-intent-to-purchase-nuclear-fuel-for-aurora-powerhouse-deployment-in-southern-ohio-302804265.html

Centrus Q1-2026 FCF negative: -$58.3M FCF for the quarter (OCF -$35.1M on inventory/working-capital builds, capex $23.2M), confirming the cash-consuming-build thesis — https://www.stocktitan.net/sec-filings/LEU/10-q-centrus-energy-corp-quarterly-earnings-report-bcd93b464f33.html

Stockanalysis/Yahoo (2026-06-21): 52-wk high $464.25, price ~$190 (~59% off high) yet ~70x P/E — the AI-power option ran hard, partly de-rated, still richly valued — https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/leu/

scale.role: Domestic HALEU enrichment (the only US producer); $900M DOE task order + Oklo LOI.
scale.rationale: Strategically scarce (sole US HALEU enricher, $3.9B backlog) — a real TACTICAL franchise on national-security scarcity, but revenue hinges on un-finalized contracts, so it stays speculative. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS, and the correction cuts mildly FOR the name (Q1 revenue actually GREW ~5%, not flat; FY26 guide raised to $450-500M), so a ~8pt haircut off a ~42 franchise. The load-bearing catalysts are non-binding (DOE order needs a definitive agreement; Oklo is an LOI with 2029 deliveries), base ~$150 ~22% below spot; wait.
scale.demand_durability: Low-now/high-optional — revenue hinges on un-finalized government/SMR contracts.
